text: This is Claudius Conrad.
Welcome to the Hepatopancreatic Biliary Surgery Forum.
In this video we will demonstrate a laparoscopic right hepatectomy.
Here we show the 12 mm port coming in right at the costal margin.
Notice the distance between this port and the liver.
With the ultrasound, you're not only ruling out lesions in the future liver remnant, you're also identifying the middle hepatic vein as a landmark for a later parenchymal transection.
In order to lower the highlight plate, we will divide cystic duct and cystic artery.
However, we recommend leaving the gallbladder itself attached to the liver, which serves as a great handle.
Here you can see the opening of the hilar plate itself, which usually leads to some minor bleeding.
This step is necessary to be able to come easily around the bile duct later during the portal dissection.
We place a hemostatic agent into the crevice after opening the hilar plate in order to avoid blood in the portal structures for later excellent visualization during the dissection.
Here we open the hepatododinal ligament at its lateral portion.
This will allow us to dissect out the right hepatic artery.
Dividing the right hepatic artery at this step will open up the space that will facilitate dissecting out the right portal vein.
The small right anterior and right posterior hepatic artery will be controlled using thermofusion and later with a clip.
The right hepatic artery is controlled using a locking clip.
The next step will be dissecting out the right portal vein branch.
This is the dissection at the bifurcation between right and left portal vein.
Notice also the closeness of the left hepatic artery at this point of the dissection.
As we get closer to the portal vein, we will switch to scissors for dissection.
This allows for more tactile feedback and an injury with scissors rather than with an energy device can be much easier controlled.
Using scissors, the portal vein is now separated from the glissonian sheath at the inferior portion.
Using a 5 mm dissector now, the portal vein is completely mobilized.
We place a suture around the right portal vein in order to be able to have traction on it, which facilitates placing the locking clip.
Please be careful with branches of the right portal vein directly into the corded lobe.
Injury to those branches can be difficult to control.
Here we control those branches with thermal fusion.
This is the view along the IVC.
You will encounter direct branches into the IVC which need to be controlled.
In order to avoid any surprises, please check on preoperative imaging for right inferior hepatic vein.
An additional short hepatic vein branch will be controlled using thermofusion.
The next step will be transaction of the paracavit portion of the caudate lobe also known as quinoa segment 9.
Due to the augmented view of the laparoscopic camera, this part might be done easier laparoscopically than open.
Doing this step early during the operation will facilitate controlling the bile duct.
We are placing now a clamp behind the bile duct in the direction of the later stapling.
The right bile duct is staple divided.
Please make sure that you leave enough distance to the takeoff of the left bile duct in order to prevent postoperative stenosis.
At this point of the operation, all portal structures to the right side have been controlled and we are moving on now to the parenchyma transaction.
Our technique for the parenchyma transaction is to activate the energy divide outside the parenchyma and keeping it activated while closing.
At this location we encounter the middle hepatic vein branch to segment 5.
It is easier to get lost with the transaction plane in the two dimensions of the laparoscopic view.
Having the IVC in view or keeping the falciform ligament attached can help to guide the parenchyma transaction plane.
Another crucial landmark is the middle hepatic vein, which was identified using ultrasound at the beginning of the case.
This is very helpful if you are more inside the parenchyma.
Because the power cable portion of the corded lobe was transected earlier, we can connect now the plane between the middle paddock vane and the IVC.
Once we get closer to the origin of the middle hepatic vein, we should look out for a segment 8 branch.
We strongly recommend also identifying this branch on preoperative imaging.
The flat IVC and the respiratory variations that can be seen are indicative of a low CVP.
Dissecting out the hepatic vein origin is always done using scissors.
This last step of the operation is always done with scissors because an injury with scissors can be much easier controlled than an injury with an energy device.
Now the right hepatic vein is completely dissected out and we will divide it using a stapler.
We recommend always having a vascular clamp as seen here ready in case of stapler misfiring.
The dissection of the hepatocaval ligament is now completed.
Short hepatic veins are controlled using thermofusion.
With the vision of hepatic vein branch 8 ventral, the specimen is now completely devascularized.
The specimen needs to be detached from the triangular ligament.
Media traction of the specimen and the help of the 30° camera aids in this process.
Using Doppler, we confirm vascular in- and outflow.
